title: Daniel
author: Daniel (affirmed by Jesus in Matthew 24:15); critical scholarship dates chapters 7–12 to the Maccabean period (~165 BC)
setting: The Babylonian and early Persian courts, where Daniel and his three friends serve as exiles. The book divides into court narratives (chs. 1–6) and apocalyptic visions (chs. 7–12).
purpose: To show God's sovereignty over the pagan nations and his certain vindication of his people, calling persecuted Jews to faithful endurance in the confidence that the 'Son of Man' will receive an everlasting kingdom.
themes: God's sovereignty over history and kingdoms
The four world empires and the coming kingdom of God
The Son of Man (7:13-14) — cited by Jesus at his trial
The resurrection at the end of time

before.label: First Babylonian deportation — Daniel taken to Babylon
Nebuchadnezzar destroys Jerusalem and the temple

after.label: Fall of Babylon to Cyrus the Persian
Cyrus's decree; exiles return under Zerubbabel
Jesus claims to be the 'Son of Man' of Daniel 7

key_people.name: Daniel
key_people.role: Jewish exile who rises to administrative prominence in Babylon through God-given wisdom and dream interpretation; his uncompromising faithfulness under imperial pressure is the book's central example of covenant faithfulness

key_people.name: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego
key_people.role: Daniel's three companions whose refusal to bow to Nebuchadnezzar's statue and subsequent deliverance from the furnace (ch. 3) is the book's paradigmatic story of costly obedience

key_people.name: Nebuchadnezzar
key_people.role: The Babylonian king whose dreams Daniel interprets and who ultimately confesses the sovereignty of Israel's God (4:34–37) — the book's most dramatic conversion narrative

key_people.name: Belshazzar
key_people.role: The last Babylonian king who profanes the temple vessels and receives the handwriting on the wall — judgment falls on him that very night

key_people.name: Darius the Mede
key_people.role: The ruler under whom Daniel is thrown to the lions (ch. 6); his conversion to recognizing YHWH (6:26–27) mirrors Nebuchadnezzar's earlier confession

context: Daniel was deported to Babylon in 605 BC with the first wave of exiles and served in the court of Nebuchadnezzar and his successors. The book divides into court narratives (chs. 1–6) and apocalyptic visions (chs. 7–12). The visions use symbolic imagery to describe the succession of world empires and the eventual establishment of God's everlasting kingdom — a genre that becomes standard in Jewish apocalyptic literature and is taken up in Revelation.
christ_connection: Jesus' most common self-designation — 'Son of Man' — comes directly from Daniel 7:13–14, where 'one like a son of man' comes with the clouds of heaven to receive an everlasting dominion over all nations. This is explicitly messianic and universally sovereign language that Jesus applies to himself at both his trial (Mark 14:62) and in describing his return (Matt 24:30). The four kingdoms of Daniel 2 and 7 culminate in a kingdom 'not made with hands' that fills the whole earth — fulfilled in Christ's resurrection kingdom advancing through the gospel.

key_verses.note: 'The God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed' — the interpretation of Nebuchadnezzar's dream that frames all subsequent visions: history is moving toward God's unshakeable kingdom
'Our God whom we serve is able to deliver us... But if not, be it known to you... we will not serve your gods' — the defining statement of unconditional covenant faithfulness under pressure
'One like a son of man came with the clouds of heaven... to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom' — Jesus' primary self-designation drawn directly from this verse
'Seventy weeks are decreed about your people... to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin, and to atone for iniquity' — the most structurally complex messianic timeline in the OT

themes_detail.title: Covenant Faithfulness Under Imperial Pressure
themes_detail.text: The court narratives (chs. 1–6) are structured around a recurring challenge: the Babylonian and Persian empires demand compromises that would violate covenant loyalty to YHWH, and Daniel and his companions repeatedly refuse. The food test (ch. 1), the furnace (ch. 3), and the lions' den (ch. 6) are not merely exciting stories but theological demonstrations that God honours faithfulness and that no earthly power can ultimately enforce apostasy. For exilic and diaspora Jews (and for NT Christians living under Roman imperial cult), these stories were both encouragement and theological argument.

themes_detail.title: God's Sovereignty Over World Empires
themes_detail.text: The dream of the statue (ch. 2) and the vision of four beasts (ch. 7) cover the same ground from two perspectives: successive world powers (Babylon, Media-Persia, Greece, Rome in the traditional reading) rise and fall, but none of them achieves ultimate permanence. God 'removes kings and sets up kings' (2:21), and history's endpoint is not the triumph of any human empire but the everlasting kingdom inaugurated by the Son of Man. This is not abstract theodicy but pastoral reassurance to people living under the thumb of the greatest empires of the ancient world.

themes_detail.title: Wisdom, Dreams, and the Limits of Human Knowledge
themes_detail.text: Daniel's gift of wisdom (1:17) and dream interpretation is explicitly God-given ('mysteries are revealed to Daniel not because of any wisdom in him', 2:30). This frames the book's knowledge claims: what the wise men of Babylon cannot know by human inquiry, God reveals to his servant. The contrast between Babylonian magicians and Daniel recurs in every court scene and makes a consistent epistemological argument: genuine understanding of history and its goal requires revelation, not merely human sagacity.

themes_detail.title: Resurrection and the End of History
themes_detail.text: Daniel 12:2 is the clearest OT statement of bodily resurrection: 'Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.' This emerges in the context of the worst tribulation the people will face (12:1) and functions as the ultimate answer to the problem of theodicy: if the faithful die for their covenant loyalty, God's justice requires their vindication beyond death. Jesus applies this expectation to himself and his people, and Paul calls Christ's resurrection the 'firstfruits' of the general resurrection (1 Cor 15:20–23).
